mnemonics.title: Remember Exception Types
mnemonics.content: Faults: Hard, Mem, Bus, Usage (HMBU) - Hardfault, Memory Management, Bus Fault, Usage Fault

mnemonics.title: Priority Mask Registers
mnemonics.content: P-R-F: PRIMASK (Priority), R (Reset all), FAULTMASK (Faults) - Remember masks increasing scope

mnemonics.title: Vector Table Order
mnemonics.content: R-N-H-S-P-B-U-S: Reset, NMI, HardFault, SVCall, PendSV, SysTick, then external IRQs

acronyms.usage: Manages all exceptions and interrupts with programmable priorities in Cortex-M processors
1-bit register that masks all configurable priority interrupts when set
Defines minimum priority for interruption - blocks lower priority interrupts
Masks all faults except NMI when set, used during critical fault handling

acronyms.expansion: Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register
acronyms.usage: Configures priority grouping and system reset in System Control Block
Allows relocation of vector table to different memory location at runtime
Software interrupt triggered by SVC instruction for OS services

visualTips.visualization: Think of priority as floor numbers in a building: Priority 0 = Ground floor (highest), Priority 15 = Top floor (lowest). Lower number = higher urgency.
Imagine a waterfall: PRIMASK acts as a complete dam, BASEPRI is a selective filter allowing only higher-priority (lower number) water through, FAULTMASK blocks everything except NMI stream.
Stack diagram: Interrupt request → cycle 1 → ... → cycle 12 → push state → handler starts. The 12-cycle path shows what happens in between.
Two conveyor belts: Instead of belt A stopping, belt B starting, belt A's end connects directly to belt B's start - no gap.

analogies.analogy: Like a hospital ER triage: cardiac arrest (highest priority/NMI), serious injuries (high priority), minor cuts (low priority). Doctors always handle higher priority first.
You're watching a movie (low priority task) when your boss calls (high priority interrupt) - you pause movie and answer call immediately.
Two ambulances arrive at same time - one with critical patient gets served first, even though both are emergencies.
At checkout: Customer A finishes, customer B is already waiting - no delay, B goes directly to checkout without cashier reset.
